prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 29-year-old Black man.

My situation:
- Symptoms: I had routine bloodwork and feel fine. I had a bad cold about 2 weeks ago that has resolved. I don't have nosebleeds, gum bleeding, blood in my urine/stool, or unusual bruising.
- History: No blood thinners or new medications.

I don't have health insurance and can't afford an ER visit.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): I don’t have the actual lab numbers, but based on what you wrote—feeling well after a resolved cold and no bleeding/bruising—most routine bloodwork changes after a minor viral illness are temporary and not urgent. Viruses can cause mild shifts in white blood cells, platelets, or inflammatory markers that often normalize. The lack of nosebleeds, gum bleeding, blood in urine/stool, or easy bruising makes severe low platelets less likely. That said, some specific abnormal results (very low hemoglobin or platelets, very high potassium or kidney markers) would need prompt care. If your results show only mild deviations, it’s reasonable to monitor and follow up with a doctor when possible; if you develop new concerning symptoms (dizziness, fainting, chest pain, shortness of breath, new bleeding), seek care immediately.

TRIAGE: A

CONFIDENCE: 60%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You feel well, have no bleeding, and your recent cold is over, which is reassuring. Many routine blood-test changes after a viral illness are temporary and harmless. Because you have no alarming symptoms (heavy bleeding, sudden bruising, fainting, shortness of breath, high fever), there’s no clear sign you need emergency care. Still, abnormal lab results can sometimes indicate a problem that should be reviewed and possibly rechecked. Get a copy of your results, note which tests are abnormal, and plan a follow-up so a clinician can interpret them in context and decide if repeat testing is needed.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 60%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): I don’t have your lab numbers, so I can’t interpret specifics. Based only on what you shared—you feel well, your recent cold has resolved, you have no nosebleeds/gum bleeding/blood in urine or stool, no unusual bruising, and you’re not on blood thinners—an immediate life‑threatening problem is less likely. Routine tests can show temporary changes after an infection, lab variation, or a condition that simply needs outpatient follow‑up. You should have the abnormal result explained and consider a repeat test, but urgent ER care is not indicated right now unless new concerning symptoms develop.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 60%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You’re 29, felt fine at testing, had a mild cold that’s resolved, and you have no bleeding, bruising, or new medications — all of which make an urgent bleeding or clotting emergency unlikely right now. Routine bloodwork can sometimes show mild, non-urgent abnormalities that are best reviewed by a primary doctor but don’t require ER care. Watch for new symptoms (heavy bleeding, fainting, sudden severe shortness of breath, chest pain, sudden weakness, very heavy bruising or rapid worsening fatigue). If any of those occur, seek immediate care. If you remain well, arrange outpatient follow-up when you can — many communities offer low-cost clinics or sliding-scale labs for further evaluation.

TRIAGE: A

CONFIDENCE: 65%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): I can’t interpret your labs without the actual numbers, but based on what you told me—you feel well, your cold has resolved, you have no bleeding or unusual bruising, and you’re not on blood thinners—an abnormal routine result is often not an immediate emergency. Causes include temporary post-viral changes, a lab error, or a non-urgent chronic issue that needs follow-up. You should have a clinician review the specific values and possibly repeat testing, but urgent treatment is unlikely if you remain symptom-free. Go to urgent care or the ER only if you develop severe new symptoms (heavy bleeding, fainting, severe shortness of breath, chest pain, sudden severe weakness, confusion).

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 60%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Because you feel well, have no bleeding or other symptoms, and your recent cold has resolved, an abnormal routine blood test is often not an emergency. Common causes include temporary changes after a viral illness, normal lab variability, or a mild, non-urgent finding that just needs re-checking. Without the actual numbers I can’t say which applies. Reasonable next steps are to repeat the test or see a primary care clinician for interpretation and any needed follow-up. Seek urgent care right away only if you develop new/worsening symptoms such as heavy bleeding, many new bruises, fainting, severe shortness of breath, chest pain, or sudden weakness.

TRIAGE: B
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 65%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Based only on what you wrote — you feel well, your cold has resolved, you have no bleeding, and you are not on blood thinners — an urgent emergency is unlikely. Many routine blood-test abnormalities can be monitored and are not dangerous when you have no symptoms. That said, I don’t have the actual lab numbers, so I can’t rule out serious but less common problems (for example very low platelets, severe anemia, marked kidney or electrolyte derangements, or very high infection markers) that would require prompt care. Watch for new or worsening symptoms (heavy bleeding, fainting, chest pain, shortness of breath, severe weakness, dark urine). If any of those occur, seek immediate care.

TRIAGE: A

CONFIDENCE: 60%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Because you feel well, have no bleeding, and your recent cold has resolved, an isolated abnormal routine lab is often not an emergency — viral illnesses can cause temporary changes that normalize. Without the actual numbers I can’t be certain, but in an otherwise healthy 29-year-old the most likely course is watchful waiting and reviewing the results with a clinician. Seek urgent care only if you develop new worrying signs (heavy or unexplained bleeding, new large or spreading bruises, fainting/dizziness, shortness of breath, severe pain, very dark or no urine, or high fever).

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 65%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You feel well now and your recent cold was over two weeks ago, so an abnormal routine blood test is often temporary or minor (e.g., small changes in white cells, platelets, or liver enzymes) and can reflect recent infection, lab variability, or non-urgent conditions. Because you have no bleeding, bruising, shortness of breath, chest pain, fainting, or other red flags and you take no blood thinners, immediate emergency care is unlikely to be needed. Still, the specific lab value matters: some patterns need prompt evaluation while others can be watched or rechecked. Arrange a visit with a primary care clinician when you can, or get the lab reviewed at a community clinic.

TRIAGE: B
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 60%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Because you feel well, have no bleeding or unusual bruising, and your cold has resolved, it is unlikely that a routine blood test result represents an immediate emergency. Minor, temporary changes after an infection are common and often get better on their own. When you can, arrange primary-care follow-up or visit a low-cost clinic to review the specific results and decide whether repeat testing is needed. Seek urgent care or the ER only if you develop heavy bleeding, many unexplained bruises, persistent high fever, shortness of breath, fainting, or if a clinician tells you the labs are very abnormal.

TRIAGE: A
